gauss-linking.gauss-linking::h1ugx1ir:
The formula arises naturally from Ampère’s law and Biot–Savart’s law.
Consider a steady current \(I\) flowing through a closed loop \(L\). The magnetic field at a point
\(\mathbf{r}\) is given by Biot–Savart:
gauss-linking.gauss-linking::hdn4r4y:
In 1833, Gauss discovered that the linking number of two closed curves
\(K\) and \(L\) in \(\mathbb{R}^3\) can be computed by a double integral:
gauss-linking.gauss-linking::h1jo5cbh:
By Ampère’s law, the circulation of \(\mathbf{B}\) around another loop \(K\) counts
how many times \(K\) passes through \(L\) — precisely the linking number.
gauss-linking.gauss-linking::h16w3j84:
Equivalently, the linking number is the degree of the Gauss map
\(G: T^2 \to S^2\) defined by:
gauss-linking.gauss-linking::h16yzp6f:
The degree counts (with sign) how many times the image covers the sphere.
Since degrees are integers, \(\mathrm{Lk}(K,L) \in \mathbb{Z}\).
gauss-linking.gauss-linking::hrs1ff0:
Select a link below and compute its linking number numerically via the Gauss integral.
The heatmap shows the integrand \(\frac{(\mathbf{r}_1 - \mathbf{r}_2) \cdot (d\mathbf{r}_1 \times d\mathbf{r}_2)}{|\mathbf{r}_1 - \mathbf{r}_2|^3}\) as a function of the parameters \((t,s)\).
